titleStmt.title: Letter from William George Lyon to J. G. Swift MacNeill, 10 April 1916.
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William George Lyon

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: This is a letter from William George Lyon (1868-1939), a publisher, to J. G. Swift MacNeill.The letter concerns the use of Robert Louis Stevenson's novel 'Kidnapped' on the school syllabus. In the letter Lyon states the book is unsuitable for Irish children to read, stating that it has a Scottish atmosphere and language and the children would need to learn a new language to read it. He also highlights the fact that the profit from the sales of the book will go to English and not Irish publishers. He goes on to say that the issue is on the floor of the House of Commons and that he expects the Irish Party to support it. The letter encloses a list of words in the book that Lyon believes Irish children will not understand, also a newspaper cutting of a letter to the editor of the Irish Independent regarding the same issue.John Gordon Swift MacNeill (1849 - 1926) was a jurist and politician. He was MP for South Donegal from 1887 until 1918.

msIdentifier.collection: National Archive of Ireland-Chief Secretary's Office-Registered Papers, NAI/CSO/RP/1916/5611/8225/5

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
10th April 1916. J. G. Swift MacNeil, Esq., M.P., 17, Pembroke Road , DUBLIN. Dear Mr. Swift MacNeil. As you are deeply interested in all that concerns
the literary and commercial welfare of Ireland, might I ask
your attention to the doings of the Intermediate Education
Board. The enclosed letter (of which we know nothing)
appeared in the 'Independent' of the 1st inst., and I now
know that the book referred to is Stevenson's 'Kidnapped'.
To my mind , the book is wholy unsuited for Irish children,
and, as it is not in Stevenson's best style , the atmosphere
is so Scotch that I am afraid it would be uncongenial to the
vast majority of Irish boys and girls; and to read it with
understanding they would require to learn a new language. I enclose a short selection of some of the words in
the only School Edition published, which is supposed to be an
expurgated edition for use in schools. Then of course, as
it is a copyright book, the whole of the profits on the sale
must go to England, and poor Irish publishers and printers
(like Mother Hubbard's dog) get none. The Programme is on the Table of the House of Commons,
and there it lies for 40 days, during which time any Member of
Parliament can take exception to any of the conditions, and I
fancy that any question regarding a subject like this would be
supported by the whole of the Irish Party.
J. G. Swift MacNeil, Esq., M.P I should be very glad if you could see your way
to bring the matter before The Chief Secretary, because if
an alternative book is not allowed there will be a serious
revolt amongst the Principals of the Intermediate Schools.
DOUBTFUL WORDS AND PHRASES IN R. L. STEVENSON'S 'KIDNAPPED', TAKEN
FROM THE ONLY SCHOOL EDITION PUBLISHED:
Page 92 (3rd line from top) 'Be damned to your word!' says he.
SCOTCH CONUNDRUMS:
Argle-bargled Deave me Orra
Bauchled Dirdum Peewees
Begowk Dirl Pypats
Birling Donnered auld runt Redd up
Boggle E Dunt Roupet
Boumen Gars Shambles E
Breeks Girning Spate - Irish Spate
Buckie Gomeral Taigle
Canny goer Gyte Thole - E
Chancy Jouk Tirls
Chapping Kyte Tod
Clour Limmer Unco
Cocking Loon (standard prose) Wame
Collops Lug Whaups
Cutty Pipe Mair Wheen
Wits upon the honey
Wyte.<pb n="3824' facs="#L1916_1479_img_3824"/> THE INTERMEDIATE.
SCOTTISH TEXT-BOOKS.
To the Editor 'Irish Independent'. Sir—It is understood that in their programme
for 1917 the Commissioners of Intermediate Education
have gone out of their way, for the second
time within three years, to prescribe a Scottish
copyright novel as the prose text for study in
the Junior Grade. The result of this action is
to prevent the book from being published in
Ireland as a school edition, unless possibly in
some arrangement which would involve the payment
of considerable amounts to the English
holders of the copyright. This running after
novelties in the form of current Scottish fiction,
in preference to the approved standard material
which exists so abundantly, might easily be shown
to be unsound educationally. It looks like the
rather belated survival of a certain literary cult
which has already exhausted itself on the other
side of the Channel. But, whatever be the intellectual motives, the
economic aspect of the matter is of more immediate
importance to the public. From that point
of view it is really intolerable that money which
might well be expended for the benefit of a
stricken Irish industry should be heedlessly sent
out of the country to swell the fees of London
copyright-owners and printers. The difficulties
of the present time are pressing with extreme
severity on the Irish printing and publishing
business. Unemployment exists on a grevious
scale; and now through the ill-advised decision
of the Intermediate Commissioners or their ad-
visers, numbers of Irish compositors, machinists,
bookbinders, girls engaged in folding and
stitching, and other workers will be deprived, this
season, of the much-needed earnings on which
they could hitherto count in the bringing out of
the Junior Grade English texts for the schools. IRISH BOOK PRODUCTION.
No one can pretend that the Commissioners are
under any obligation to depart from an estab-
lished custom, or to look to Scotland and its
copyrights for any new literary inspiration. The
vast range of standard, non-copyright English
prose is still open to them as ever; and surely
this illimitable field is adequate enough to provide suffi-
client basis of selection to satisfy every possible
taste or requirement, and thus enable the books
to be produced in Ireland, as heretofore, in well-
edited texts. Public opinion, it is hoped, will be strong
enough to induce the Commissioners to abandon
their present intention in this regard. But, if
necessary it ought to be made clear that Parlia-
ment, at the instance of the Irish members, will
refuse to sanction the new rules and regulations
of the Board until the Scottish copyright novel
is removed from the programme in favour of some
suitable non-copyright book which the Irish firms
will be at liberty to reprint and publish among
their Intermediate editions. IRISHMAN <pb n="3825' facs="#L1916_1479_img_3825"/> QUESTION. To ask The Chief Secretary for Ireland if his
attention has been called to the Intermediate Programme
for 1917, in which 'Kidnapped' is prescribed for study
by Junior Grade Pupils. If so, is The Chief Secretary aware that the
book contains matter wholly unsuitable forschool studyIrish children,
inasmuch as it has no educative tendencies, and is couched
in a language which will not be understood in Ireland. Under these circumstances will The chief Secretary
recommend that an alternative book be prescribed.
